Operation Grand Slam.

[Indian Army](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/indian-army/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Operation Grand Slam.

### The 1965 War series.

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Sep 02, 2024

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!JxD2!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F16c2e3b4-9284-437d-a33f-dabaef307162_300x192.jpeg)

![apt](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!JxD2!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F16c2e3b4-9284-437d-a33f-dabaef307162_300x192.jpeg "apt")

Operation Gibraltar was a covert plan that involved infiltrating Jammu and Kashmir with disguised troops to incite rebellion among the Muslim-majority population. The hope was to trigger an uprising that would justify a larger military intervention.

The operation backfired. India swiftly mobilized, not only repelling the infiltrators but launching counter-offensives that led to the capture of the **Haji Pir Pass**, a critical route through the Pir Panjal range.While Pakistan gained temporary control over **Uri and Poonch**, India’s hold over Haji Pir was a tactical win. It allowed better access and surveillance over infiltration routes.

Following Gibraltar’s failure, Pakistan launched **Operation Grand Slam** targeting Akhnoor directly. The goal was to exploit the surprise and break through Indian defenses.

Akhnoor is believed to be the site of the *Virat Nagari* from the Mahabharata, where the Pandavas spent their incognito year. The town’s fort, perched on the Chenab’s banks, echoes centuries of layered history, from ancient dynasties to Dogra rule.

In 1965, Akhnoor wasn’t just a sleepy town, it was a vital link between Jammu and the Kashmir Valley. Its capture would have allowed Pakistan to sever Indian supply lines and potentially open a corridor into the heart of Jammu.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!hKIO!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F704a677d-377f-4a5c-b74d-21cca8785c32_263x300.png)

![ch](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!hKIO!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F704a677d-377f-4a5c-b74d-21cca8785c32_263x300.png "ch")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Ayub Khan’s infamous remark about “Hindu morale” reflected a dangerously simplistic view of Indian resolve. It was rooted in a belief that swift, decisive strikes could demoralize India and force a favorable political outcome.

This mindset shaped the planning of **Operation Grand Slam**, which followed the failure of Gibraltar. The goal was to strike Akhnoor, sever Indian supply lines to Kashmir, and pressure India into negotiation.

At the time, Akhnoor was lightly defended by just **4 Indian infantry battalions, 1 squadron of AMX-13 light tanks and 3 artillery batteries.**

In contrast, Pakistan deployed, **8 infantry battalions,6 squadrons of Patton tanks** that were far superior in armor and firepower and **18 artillery batteries,** including **8-inch guns** that outclassed Indian field artillery

Ayub Khan famously pointed to Akhnoor on the map and called it India’s “jugular,” believing its capture would sever supply lines to Kashmir. In reality, **NH1A**, the arterial route to Srinagar, ran through **Udhampur and Ramban**, bypassing Akhnoor entirely. This miscalculation shaped Pakistan’s operational goals but undermined their strategic payoff, even if Akhnoor fell, India’s logistics would remain intact.

Gen Akhtar Malik, a seasoned tactician, understood the limitations of Ayub’s plan. He secretly devised a **follow-up thrust toward Jammu**, which *would* have threatened NH1A and Indian rear echelons. However, these plans were **not disclosed to Ayub Khan**, likely to avoid provoking a full-scale war,something Pakistan’s leadership was keen to avoid.

**Operation Gibraltar** was meant to be a covert insurgency, while **Grand Slam** was a conventional assault. The ambiguity over whether Grand Slam was a backup or a parallel prong led to, **poor coordination,unclear objectives and command confusion**, especially with the abrupt replacement of Gen Malik by Gen Yahya Khan mid-operation.

Pakistan’s planners hoped to keep the conflict **localized to Kashmir**, avoiding escalation across the international border. But India’s counter-offensive into **Punjab** shattered that illusion, forcing Pakistan to abandon Grand Slam and defend its heartland.

In the **early hours of September 1**, Pakistan launched **Operation Grand Slam**, initiating a massive artillery and armored assault across the **Manawar Tawi River** into the **Chamb-Jaurian sector**. The Indian Army HQ in the region was caught off guard. The **XV Corps**, already stretched thin from counterinsurgency operations post-Gibraltar, had limited reinforcements and was still reorganizing its command structure.

The Indian defenders included, **4 infantry battalions, AMX-13 light tanks** from the **20th Lancers and** a few **artillery batteries**, including the **85th Light Battery** and **14th Field Regiment.**These units were **heavily outnumbered** and outgunned. The AMX-13s, though nimble, were no match for the **M47/M48 Patton tanks** fielded by Pakistan’s armored regiments.

Pakistan’s use of **8-inch guns** gave them a devastating edge. These heavy artillery pieces could deliver high-explosive shells with deep penetration and wide-area damage, far beyond the reach of India’s standard field guns. The **shock and awe** of this bombardment led to significant Indian casualties and forced a tactical withdrawal from forward positions.

Pakistan made rapid gains in the **Chamb-Jaurian** area, but delays,especially the controversial replacement of **Gen Akhtar Malik,** slowed the momentum. India’s broader counter-offensive across the Punjab front forced Pakistan to divert resources, preventing a deeper thrust toward **Akhnoor or Jammu**.

On **September 2**, just as the operation was gaining momentum, Pakistan’s GHQ abruptly replaced him with **Gen Yahya Khan**, commander of the **7th Infantry Division**. The reasons remain murky, some cite internal politics, others suggest discomfort with Malik’s Ahmadiya background. Whatever the motive, the timing was catastrophic.

The handover wasn’t seamless. Malik had to brief Yahya Khan, who then needed time to assess the battlefield. This **caused a full day’s delay** in the offensive, which allowed India to, **rush reinforcements** into the Chamb sector, **stabilize defensive lines, reorganize artillery and armor**, including repositioning AMX-13 tanks and field guns.

Lower-level Pakistani units, already engaged in combat, were left in **operational limbo**. Orders stalled, coordination broke down, and the momentum of Day 1 dissipated. The delay also **diluted the psychological edge** Pakistan had gained through its initial surprise and firepower.

After the chaotic first two days, the **Indian Army had reinforced the Akhnoor sector**, deploying additional infantry, artillery, and armor. Defensive positions were **dug in deeply**, especially around **Akhnoor and Jourian**, with overlapping fields of fire and better coordination between units. Though still recovering from initial losses, India had stabilized the front. However, it lacked the **armored strength and air cover** to mount a full-scale counterattack.

Pakistan resumed its offensive on **Day 3**, but the momentum was gone. The **command change** from Gen Akhtar Malik to Gen Yahya Khan had disrupted planning and morale.

Indian reinforcements had arrived, and defensive lines were now hardened. Terrain in the **Chhamb-Jaurian sector**, with its rivers and ridges, favored defenders once the element of surprise was lost.

From **September 3 to 5**, the battle settled into a **stalemate** as Pakistan’s **Patton tanks and artillery** continued to pound Indian positions, but without coordinated infantry support, gains were minimal.

India’s **AMX-13 tanks and field guns**, though technically inferior, were now better positioned and supported. Neither side could break through, and both began to suffer from **logistical strain and attrition**.

The stalemate at Akhnoor allowed India to **open a new front in Punjab**, shifting the war’s center of gravity. Pakistan, now facing pressure on multiple fronts, had to **divert resources**, effectively abandoning its original goal of capturing Akhnoor and threatening Jammu.

With the **Akhnoor sector stalemated**, India made a bold move: **crossing the International Border** into Pakistan’s heartland. The offensive was led by **Major General Niranjan Prasad**, a WWII veteran commanding the **15th Infantry Division**, tasked with advancing toward **Lahore** via the **Amritsar–Lahore axis**.

This was not just retaliation,it was a **strategic masterstroke**. By threatening Lahore, India forced Pakistan to **divert troops and armor** from Kashmir, relieving pressure on the northern front.

Prior to this, Indian forces had already **repelled Pakistani infiltrators from Kargil**, securing the strategic town and restoring control over key mountain passes.This ensured that Pakistan’s northern gambit via **Operation Gibraltar** had fully collapsed.

The **Icchogil Canal** (also called the **BRB Canal**) was Pakistan’s primary defensive line east of Lahore. It was a formidable obstacle with elevated banks, machine gun bunkers, and Anti-tank defenses.

Indian forces initially suffered setbacks here, with **Pakistani counterattacks** and **air strikes** causing disarray. Gen Prasad himself faced criticism for his conduct under fire.

Despite early reverses, Indian troops regrouped and launched a successful assault on the **village of Barki**, east of Lahore. They **secured the bridge over the canal**, pushing forward until they were **within artillery range of Lahore International Airport**—the closest any Indian unit got to the city.

The **2nd Independent Armored Brigade**, with **three tank regiments**, played a crucial role in this advance, showcasing India’s evolving armored doctrine.

The rapid Indian advance alarmed the **United States**, which requested a **temporary ceasefire** to evacuate its citizens from Lahore. This underscored the **international dimension** of the conflict, with Cold War powers watching closely.

**3 Jat Regiment**, under the indomitable **Lt Col Desmond Hayde**, crossed the **Ichhogil Canal**,a formidable defensive barrier,and seized **Batapore**, a key town near **Dograi**, just east of Lahore. This was a daring maneuver. Pakistani forces had **blown up bridges** to halt Indian advances, but 3 Jat pressed on, engaging in fierce combat and securing the town by midday.

Pakistan responded swiftly as ground **forces**, supported by **air strikes**, launched a counteroffensive. The **15th Division**, lacking coordination and reinforcements, was forced to **retreat to its original positions**.

Meanwhile, a **breakdown in communication** at Indian HQ meant that **3 Jat’s success went unnoticed**. Orders were issued for them to **withdraw to Ghosal-Dial**, despite having already secured Batapore.Lt Col Hayde, frustrated and bewildered, had to **abandon a hard-won position**, even as his men held the ground against superior odds.

Around this time, Pakistan launched a **massive armored thrust toward Khem Karan**, threatening India’s southern flank. This forced Indian command to **divert troops and armor** from the Lahore front, weakening the offensive momentum and shifting focus to defensive operations.

Two weeks later, **3 Jat was ordered to retake Batapore and Dograi**, now heavily fortified by Pakistan. Hayde’s men marched 8 km under fire, engaging in **hand-to-hand combat**, and finally secured the town in a **bloody battle** that cost 86 Indian lives but inflicted over 300 Pakistani casualties. This second capture was a testament to **discipline, grit, and leadership**, earning Hayde the **Maha Vir Chakra** and cementing 3 Jat’s legacy.
